questions.explanation: Metrics (aggregated numbers), Logs (discrete events), Traces (request paths through services). Together answer: how fast / what happened / where did time go.

questions.q: SLO of 99.9% monthly. How many minutes of downtime does that allow?
questions.explanation: 30 days × 0.001 = 43.2 min/month. 99.99% = ~4.3 min. Every 9 is a ~10× smaller budget. The SLO directly sets error-budget-driven on-call responses.

questions.q: p99 latency of 500ms but p50 is 50ms. What's the implication?
choices: Most users see sub-100ms; a meaningful minority see slow tail
questions.explanation: p50 is the middle; p99 is the 99th-percentile worst. 1 in 100 users hits 500ms. In a 100-call page, that's near-certain to happen once. Tail latency is what users feel.

questions.q: Distributed tracing: what does a 'span' represent?
choices: One unit of work (RPC, DB query) within a trace, with start/end timestamps
questions.explanation: Span = a single operation. Many spans form a trace. Parent-child relationships show call hierarchy. Shows latency breakdown: where is the time going?

questions.q: An alert fires every time CPU > 80%. Why is this often a bad SLO alert?
choices: It's a CAUSE metric not a SYMPTOM; users don't care about CPU directly
questions.explanation: SLO alerts should be user-symptom-based: high p99 latency, high error rate, low success rate. CPU might just be healthy load. Alerting on symptoms + investigating causes scales better than alerting on causes.

questions.q: Sampling: trace 0.1% of requests. Why not 100%?
choices: Storage + compute cost prohibitive at scale
questions.explanation: At 100k QPS × full tracing = petabytes/day of data. Typical: 0.1–1% head sampling (decide at root) or tail sampling (keep all traces with errors/high-latency).

questions.q: An error budget for 99.9% SLO over 28 days is spent. What's the right response?
choices: Freeze non-critical launches; focus on reliability work until budget recovers
questions.explanation: Error budget = allowable downtime. Spent = user experience already degraded. Freezing feature work + prioritizing reliability work is the standard Google SRE response.

questions.q: A developer adds a Prometheus metric with labels {user_id, endpoint, status_code}. With 10M users and 50 endpoints, what problem will this cause?
choices: Cardinality explosion: 10M x 50 x ~5 status codes = 2.5 billion time series, overwhelming Prometheus storage and query performance
No problem — Prometheus handles any cardinality
questions.explanation: High-cardinality labels (like user_id) create a unique time series per combination. Prometheus stores each series separately. Billions of series exhaust memory and make queries timeout. Fix: remove user_id from metric labels; use logs or traces for per-user debugging. Metrics should use bounded-cardinality labels only.

questions.q: Your on-call engineer receives 47 alerts in 10 minutes during a cascading failure. They can't identify the root cause amid the noise. What observability practice would have helped?
choices: Alert grouping/deduplication and dependency-aware alerting: group related alerts, suppress downstream symptoms, surface only the root-cause alert
questions.explanation: Alert fatigue is a leading cause of slow incident response. Alertmanager-style grouping (by service, cluster) and inhibition rules (if database-down fires, suppress all dependent service alerts) reduce noise. The goal: one actionable alert per root cause, not one per symptom.

questions.q: You sample traces at 1% (head-based sampling). A rare but critical payment failure happens on 0.01% of requests. Your traces almost never capture these failures. What sampling strategy fixes this?
choices: Tail-based sampling: buffer all traces briefly, then keep traces that contain errors, high latency, or specific flags regardless of head-sampling rate
questions.explanation: Head-based sampling decides at the start of a request whether to trace it — before you know if it will fail. Tail-based sampling (e.g., OpenTelemetry Collector with tail_sampling processor) evaluates completed traces and retains interesting ones (errors, slow, specific attributes). This captures rare failures without the cost of 100% tracing.

questions.q: Your p99 latency alert fires but your dashboard shows average latency is fine at 45ms. The team debates whether there's a real problem. Who is right?
choices: Average is fine, ignore the alert
The p99 alert is correct: averages hide tail latency; 1% of users are experiencing severe latency that the average masks
p99 is always noisy, disable it
questions.explanation: Averages are misleading for latency. A 45ms average can hide a p99 of 5 seconds. With 1000 requests/sec, that's 10 users/sec hitting multi-second latency. At scale, every page load likely includes at least one p99-level backend call. Alert on percentiles (p95/p99), not averages.

questions.q: After enabling structured JSON logging across all services, your log storage costs jumped from $2K/month to $18K/month. Searches are also slower. What's the most effective cost reduction that preserves debugging ability?
choices: Switch back to plain text logs
Implement log levels + sampling: DEBUG/INFO logs sampled at 10%, WARN/ERROR at 100%; add a dynamic log-level override for on-demand debugging of specific services
questions.explanation: Most log volume is DEBUG/INFO for healthy requests. Sampling low-severity logs (or dropping them entirely in production) cuts volume dramatically. Keep 100% of WARN/ERROR. Dynamic log-level overrides let you temporarily enable DEBUG for a specific service during incidents. This is the standard approach at high-scale companies.
